beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. An agent with 200 features to implement. It completes 50 in session one and the context window fills. Session two starts blank. How does it know to begin at feature 51 — without replaying the first 50?

props.command: Externalize my agent's progress to a feature_list.json + git so it can resume across sessions — then prove it picks up where it left off.
outputLines: Session 2: reads feature_list.json, finds item 51 incomplete.

beats.narration_text: A context window has a limit. When the agent's conversation fills it, the session ends. Next session: blank. No memory of what was done, what passed, what failed. The agent has two choices — re-read everything, which takes half the new context — or guess where it left off. Neither is good.

props.command: Session 1 fills context after 50 features. Session 2 starts blank. No memory. Options: re-read everything (costs half context) or guess (wrong).

viz.description: A session timeline on cream stage. Session 1: a filled bar labeled '50 features — context full'. A vertical divider labeled 'Session boundary'. Session 2: an empty bar labeled 'blank slate'. Two arrows branch from Session 2: one toward 'Re-read everything' (dim, costly), one toward 'Guess' (red X). Both are wrong. Source: Claude Quickstarts (Anthropic).

beats.act: the fix — feature_list.json as external state
beats.narration_text: The fix: two external files. feature underscore list dot json is the source of truth — 200 entries, each with an ID and a status: incomplete or passing. Git is the immutable ledger — a commit per feature, so history cannot be disputed. Each session reads the file, finds the first incomplete entry, implements it, runs tests, commits, marks it passing. Then repeats.

props.command:
# Each session:
features = json.load(open('feature_list.json'))
next_item = next(f for f in features if not f['passes'])
# implement, test, commit, mark passing

beats.act: centerpiece — the feature list mutating across sessions
beats.narration_text: Here is the feature underscore list dot json mutating in real time. Session 1: 200 items, all incomplete. Features 1 through 50 flip one by one — incomplete to passing. A git commit marks each one. Session boundary. Session 2 opens the file, finds item 51 as the first incomplete, and starts there. By the end of session 2, items 51 through 100 are passing. The file is the memory. Source: Claude Quickstarts, Anthropic.

viz.description: A vertical list of feature rows on cream stage, each showing id and status badge. Status badges animate from 'incomplete' (INK_SOFT) to 'passing' (terracotta checkmark). Rows 1-50 flip to passing during 'Session 1'. A terracotta vertical line labeled 'Session boundary' divides the list. Rows 51-100 start flipping in 'Session 2'. At the bottom: a git log column showing commit hashes accumulate as each feature passes. Source: Claude Quickstarts (Anthropic).

beats.act: honesty beat — what this does NOT cover
beats.narration_text: What this does not cover: how the agent generates the initial feature list — that is the initializer agent's job, which is a separate session. And it does not cover the detailed test framework that marks items passing. This video is the checkpoint-and-resume mechanism: the file, the git ledger, the session boundary, and the first-incomplete lookup.

props.command: Exclusions: how the initializer generates the feature list; detailed testing framework. This: the checkpoint-and-resume mechanism only.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. An agent's context window is not its memory — it's a workspace. When the workspace fills, the work stops. The fix: externalize state. Feature underscore list dot json holds the truth; git holds the history. Each new session reads, finds the first gap, and fills it. The agent never starts over — it just opens the file.

artifactLines: A context window is a workspace, not memory — when it fills, the session ends and the next one starts blank.
feature_list.json externalizes state: 200 entries, each with an id and a passes flag the agent updates after each test.
Git commits are the immutable ledger: each passing feature gets a commit, so history can't be disputed across sessions.
Each new session reads the file, finds the first incomplete entry, and resumes there — no rework, no guessing.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The prompt: 'Externalize my agent's progress to a feature underscore list dot json plus git so it can resume across sessions — then prove it picks up where it left off.' That prompt asks Claude to write the full harness. Does it handle partial test failures? Does it commit before or after marking passing? Does it guard against double-counting? Run it. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Externalize my agent's progress to a feature_list.json + git so it can resume across sessions — then prove it picks up where it left off.
